questions.text: Who drafted the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution.

questions.text: The Magna Carta was signed in 1215 and limited the powers of the English King.
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was indeed signed in 1215 by King John.

questions.text: Which article of the Indian Constitution grants Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Articles 12–35 of the Indian Constitution outline the Fundamental Rights granted to all citizens.

questions.text: In which year did India achieve independence from British rule?
questions.explanation: India gained independence on August 15, 1947.

questions.text: The French Revolution occurred in the 18th century.
questions.explanation: The French Revolution took place from 1789 to 1799.

questions.text: The Indian Constitution was adopted on ______ 26, 1950.
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution came into effect on January 26, 1950, which is celebrated as Republic Day.

questions.text: Who was the first President of independent India?
questions.explanation: Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India from 1950 to 1962.

questions.text: Which of the following are Fundamental Rights granted by the Indian Constitution? (Select all correct)
options: B. Right to Freedom of Religion
questions.explanation: Rights to Equality, Freedom of Religion, and Constitutional Remedies are Fundamental Rights. Exploitation and unlimited wealth are not rights granted.

questions.text: Assertion: The French Revolution influenced democratic movements worldwide. Reason: It established ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity.
questions.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are correct. The French Revolution's principles inspired global democratization.

questions.text: Who was known as the 'Father of the Indian Nation'?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi is revered as the Father of the Indian Nation for his role in independence.

questions.text: The American Revolution occurred before the French Revolution.
questions.explanation: The American Revolution (1775–1783) preceded the French Revolution (1789–1799).

questions.text: The doctrine of Satyagraha, or non-violent resistance, was championed by ______.
questions.explanation: Satyagraha, meaning truth and non-violence, was Gandhi's powerful tool against British colonialism.

questions.text: Which ancient civilization built the Great Wall?
questions.explanation: China built the Great Wall over many centuries to protect against invasions.

questions.text: Match historical figures with their major contributions:
pairs: End of apartheid in South Africa
questions.explanation: Each historical figure made significant contributions to their respective nations and the world.

questions.text: In which year was the Preamble to the Indian Constitution adopted?
questions.explanation: The Preamble to the Indian Constitution was adopted on November 26, 1949.

questions.text: The British East India Company was primarily a military organization.
questions.explanation: The British East India Company was a trading company that eventually became a colonial power.

questions.text: The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 symbolized the end of the ______ War.
questions.explanation: The fall of the Berlin Wall marked the symbolic end of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and Western powers.

questions.text: Which empire built the Colosseum in Rome?
questions.explanation: The Colosseum was built during the Roman Empire, completed in 80 AD.

questions.text: Assertion: The American Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776. Reason: It proclaimed the thirteen colonies' independence from British rule.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Declaration of Independence was signed on July 4, 1776.

questions.text: Which of the following were key events in the Indian Independence Movement? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: The Salt March, Quit India Movement, and Rebellion of 1857 were key independence events. Plassey was a British victory, and Versailles was post-WWI.

questions.text: Who wrote the American Constitution?
options: C. James Madison and the Founding Fathers
questions.explanation: The American Constitution was drafted by James Madison and other Founding Fathers at the Constitutional Convention of 1787.

questions.text: The Renaissance period in Europe occurred during the Middle Ages.
questions.explanation: The Renaissance (14th–17th centuries) followed the Middle Ages and marked the transition to modern Europe.

questions.text: The Treaty of ______ ended World War I in 1918.
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919, formally ending WWI.

questions.text: Which ancient wonder of the world still stands today?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the only ancient wonder still largely intact.

questions.text: Assertion: The Industrial Revolution transformed economies from agrarian to industrial. Reason: It introduced mechanization and factory systems.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Industrial Revolution fundamentally changed economic and social structures.

questions.text: Match empires with their characteristics:
pairs: Emperor Ashoka and early Indian unity
questions.explanation: These empires left distinct cultural, architectural, and political legacies in India.

questions.text: In which year did the Jallianwala Bagh massacre occur?
questions.explanation: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on April 13, 1919, in Amritsar.

questions.text: The Indus Valley Civilization is one of the world's oldest known civilizations.
questions.explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization (2600–1900 BCE) is one of the world's oldest urban civilizations.

questions.text: The ______ Dynasty ruled the Mughal Empire and left a lasting impact on Indian architecture.
questions.explanation: The Mughal Dynasty ruled from 1526 to 1857 and built iconic structures like the Taj Mahal.

questions.text: Who was the first Emperor of the Maurya Empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya Empire around 322 BCE.

questions.text: Which of the following were significant women in Indian history? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Rani Lakshmibai, Savitribai Phule, and Indira Gandhi were significant Indian historical figures. Queen Victoria was British.

questions.text: Assertion: The Vedic period is the foundation of Hindu civilization. Reason: The Vedas were the first sacred texts written in Sanskrit.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Vedas form the basis of Hindu philosophy and Indian culture.

questions.text: Which movement advocated for Swadeshi (self-reliance) during the Indian Independence struggle?
questions.explanation: The Swadeshi Movement encouraged Indians to use indigenous products and boycott British goods.

questions.text: The Roman Empire fell completely in 476 AD.
questions.explanation: The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 AD, marking the end of the classical period.

questions.text: The ______ Edict of Ashoka promoted Buddhist teachings across his empire.
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Pillars contained edicts that spread Buddhist teachings and moral teachings throughout the Maurya Empire.

questions.text: In which battle did the British defeat Tipu Sultan?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Seringapatam (1799) resulted in Tipu Sultan's defeat and death.

questions.text: Assertion: The partition of India in 1947 created the nations of India and Pakistan. Reason: Religious and communal tensions necessitated the division.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Partition was driven by communal differences and led to massive population displacement.

questions.text: Match important Indian historical dates with events:
pairs: Formation of the Indian National Congress
questions.explanation: These dates mark pivotal moments in India's journey toward independence and nation-building.

questions.text: Who was the first Prime Minister of India?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru served as the first Prime Minister of India from 1947 to 1964.

questions.text: The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg in the 15th century.
questions.explanation: Gutenberg invented the movable type printing press around 1440, revolutionizing information dissemination.

questions.text: The ______ Pact of 1942 was a failed attempt to resolve political deadlock during India's independence struggle.
questions.explanation: The Cripps Mission of 1942 proposed dominion status but was rejected by Indian leaders seeking complete independence.

questions.text: Which treaty ended the Napoleonic Wars?
questions.explanation: The Congress of Vienna (1815) and resulting treaties ended the Napoleonic Wars.

questions.text: Assertion: The Enlightenment period promoted reason and scientific thinking. Reason: Philosophers like Voltaire and Rousseau challenged traditional authority.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Enlightenment thinkers advocated rationalism and individual liberty.

questions.text: Which of the following were features of the Mughal administrative system? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Mansabdari system, Persian language, and centralized bureaucracy were key features. Federal and democratic elements were absent.

questions.text: Who led the Revolt of 1857 from Kanpur?
questions.explanation: Nana Sahib led the rebellion from Kanpur and was one of the revolt's major leaders.

questions.text: The Western Roman Empire and Eastern Byzantine Empire coexisted for centuries.
questions.explanation: After 395 AD, the Roman Empire was divided, with the Byzantine Empire lasting until 1453.

questions.text: The ______ was the most significant anti-colonial movement launched by Gandhi in 1942.
questions.explanation: The Quit India Movement demanded immediate British withdrawal from India.

questions.text: Which ruler unified most of India under the Maurya Empire?
questions.explanation: Emperor Ashoka expanded the Maurya Empire to its greatest extent, ruling most of the Indian subcontinent.

questions.text: Assertion: The Magna Carta established the principle that the king is subject to law. Reason: It limited royal power through written agreements.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Magna Carta was foundational to constitutional governance.

questions.text: Match historical figures with their respective nations:
questions.explanation: These leaders were instrumental in shaping their nations during periods of major political change.

questions.text: Who authored the book 'The Discovery of India'?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru wrote 'The Discovery of India' while imprisoned during the independence struggle.

questions.text: The American Civil War was fought between 1861 and 1865.
questions.explanation: The American Civil War occurred from 1861 to 1865, resulting in the abolition of slavery.

questions.text: Which of the following were significant revolts against British rule in India? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Sepoy Mutiny, Moplah Rebellion, Khilafat Movement, and Civil Disobedience Movement were significant revolts. Treaty of Amritsar was a peace treaty.

questions.text: Which ancient Indian empire was known for its advancement in mathematics and astronomy?
questions.explanation: The Gupta Empire (320-550 CE) is renowned for its scientific and cultural achievements, including the invention of zero.

questions.text: The Khilafat Movement was launched to support the Ottoman Caliphate.
questions.explanation: The Khilafat Movement (1920-1924) was an Indian Muslim movement supporting the Ottoman Caliphate.

questions.text: The ______ Empire was the longest-lasting empire in medieval South India.
questions.explanation: The Chola Empire ruled South India from around 9th to 13th centuries and was known for maritime trade.

questions.text: Which of the following were contributions of the Gupta Empire? (Select all correct)
options: E. Creation of the Indian Penal Code
questions.explanation: Gupta Empire contributed to decimal system, Sanskrit literature, and algebra. Mahabalipuram temples were Pallava, and IPC was British.

questions.text: Assertion: The Delhi Sultanate replaced the Hindu kingdoms in North India. Reason: Islamic rulers established their dominance through military conquests.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Delhi Sultanate (1206-1526) marked Islamic rule over North India.

questions.text: Who was the architect of the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: Ustad Ahmed Lahauri was the chief architect who designed the Taj Mahal under Shah Jahan's patronage.

questions.text: Match medieval Indian rulers with their empires:
questions.explanation: These rulers were dominant figures in their respective medieval Indian empires.

questions.text: The Reformation movement was primarily led by Martin Luther in Europe.
questions.explanation: Martin Luther's 95 Theses (1517) initiated the Protestant Reformation in Europe.

questions.text: The ______ Rebellion was the first major uprising against British rule in India.
questions.explanation: The Sepoy Mutiny of 1857 was triggered by the introduction of new cartridges.

questions.text: Which British official was responsible for the partition of Bengal?
questions.explanation: Lord Curzon, the Viceroy, partitioned Bengal in 1905, leading to the Swadeshi Movement.

questions.text: Which of the following were published works by Indian independence leaders? (Select all correct)
options: C. 'A Nation in Making' by Subhas Chandra Bose
D. 'The Economic History of India' by Romesh Chunder Dutt
questions.explanation: Gandhi, Bose, and Dutt wrote on independence and economics. Valmiki and Tulsidas were ancient/medieval poets.

questions.text: Assertion: Subhas Chandra Bose believed in armed struggle for independence. Reason: He diverged from Gandhi's non-violent approach.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Subhas Chandra Bose advocated for armed resistance and formed the Indian National Army.

questions.text: Which movement led to the Non-Cooperation with the British?
questions.explanation: The Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-1922) was launched by Gandhi to boycott British institutions.

questions.text: The ______ Act of 1935 provided India with a federal structure for the first time.
questions.explanation: The Government of India Act of 1935 established a federal framework for Indian governance.

questions.text: The Cabinet Mission Plan was sent to India in 1945 to discuss independence.
questions.explanation: The Cabinet Mission of 1946 attempted to negotiate a framework for Indian independence.

questions.text: Match Indian independence leaders with their roles:
questions.explanation: These leaders played crucial roles in India's independence and nation-building.

questions.text: Which ancient Indian city was a major center of the Indus Valley Civilization?
questions.explanation: Harappa was one of the two major cities of the Indus Valley Civilization, alongside Mohenjo-daro.

questions.text: The Vedas were composed in Sanskrit during the Vedic period.
questions.explanation: The four Vedas are the oldest sacred texts of Hinduism, composed in Vedic Sanskrit.

questions.text: The ______ Edicts were inscribed by Emperor Ashoka to spread his message of dharma.
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Rock Edicts were inscribed on rocks and pillars throughout his empire.

questions.text: Which of the following were achievements of the Pallava dynasty? (Select all correct)
options: E. Naval expansion to Southeast Asia
questions.explanation: Pallavas contributed to Dravidian architecture, temple construction, literature, and art. Naval expansion was more associated with Cholas.

questions.text: Assertion: The Rashtrakuta Empire controlled large parts of central India. Reason: They fought the Tripartite Struggle with the Cholas and Pratiharas.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Rashtrakuta-Chola-Pratihara conflict shaped medieval Indian politics.

questions.text: Who was the Portuguese explorer who established the first European settlement in India?
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama arrived in India in 1498, marking the beginning of direct European contact.

questions.text: Match colonial powers with their Indian settlements:
questions.explanation: Different European powers established settlements in various parts of India.

questions.text: The Battle of Plassey was fought between the British and Bengali forces in 1757.
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey in 1757 established British supremacy over Bengal under Robert Clive.

questions.text: The ______ War was fought between Britain and France for control of India.
questions.explanation: The Carnatic Wars (1746-1763) between British and French shaped colonial India's future.

questions.text: Which organization was founded in 1885 to represent Indian political interests?
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress was founded on December 28, 1885, as the first major political organization.

questions.text: Which of the following were leaders of the Indian National Congress? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: All except Jinnah were Congress leaders. Jinnah later led the Muslim League and Pakistan movement.

questions.text: Assertion: The Indian Rebellion of 1857 was brutally suppressed by the British. Reason: It was perceived as a major threat to British colonial rule.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The rebellion led to significant changes in British administration of India.

questions.text: Which reform movement sought to modernize Hindu society in the 19th century?
questions.explanation: All three movements contributed to social and religious reform in 19th century India.

questions.text: The ______ Movement promoted the use of Indian languages and boycott of English education.
questions.explanation: The Swadeshi Movement emphasized indigenous culture and economic self-sufficiency.

questions.text: The Home Rule Movement was launched by Annie Besant and Bal Gangadhar Tilak.
questions.explanation: The Home Rule Movement (1916-1918) demanded self-governance for India during World War I.

questions.text: Match social reformers with their contributions:
pairs: Abolition of sati and women's education
questions.explanation: These reformers addressed critical social issues in 19th century India.

questions.text: Which British policy aimed at anglicizing Indian education and culture?
questions.explanation: Macaulay's Minute (1835) established English as the medium of education in India.

questions.text: Which of the following were consequences of the 1857 Rebellion? (Select all correct)
options: A. Abolition of the East India Company
B. Transfer of power to the British Crown
C. Expansion of British military presence
D. Increased Indian representation in administration
E. Introduction of the Doctrine of Lapse
questions.explanation: The rebellion led to direct Crown rule and military expansion. Indian representation increased much later.

questions.text: Assertion: The partition of 1947 resulted in significant communal violence. Reason: The partition occurred suddenly and millions had to migrate between nations.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Partition led to one of history's largest mass migrations and communal riots.

questions.text: Who was the first Governor-General of independent India?
questions.explanation: Lord Louis Mountbatten was the first Governor-General of independent India.

questions.text: The ______ Committee drafted the Directive Principles of State Policy in the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: The Drafting Committee, chaired by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, drafted all provisions of the Constitution.

questions.text: The Indian Constitution came into force on January 26, 1950.
questions.explanation: The Constitution was adopted on January 26, 1950, marking the establishment of the Indian Republic.

questions.text: Which of the following are features of the Indian Constitution? (Select all correct)
options: A. Federalism with separation of powers
D. Directive Principles of State Policy
questions.explanation: The Constitution establishes a federal, parliamentary democracy. Monarchy is not absolute but ceremonial.

questions.text: Assertion: The Preamble of the Indian Constitution outlines the objectives of the Constitution. Reason: It contains the fundamental ideals the Constitution seeks to establish.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Preamble defines the vision of the Indian Republic.

questions.text: Which article of the Constitution abolishes untouchability?
questions.explanation: Article 17 of the Indian Constitution abolishes untouchability and prohibits its practice.

questions.text: Match constitutional terms with their definitions:
pairs: No official religion; treats all religions equally
Power vested in the people through voting
Independent in making its own decisions
questions.explanation: These concepts form the foundation of the Indian constitutional framework.

questions.text: The Indian Constitution is the longest written constitution in the world.
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution is the longest constitution with over 400 articles and 12 schedules.

questions.text: The ______ Rights refer to rights that protect citizens from exploitation and promote their welfare.
questions.explanation: Socio-economic rights are embedded in the Directive Principles of State Policy.

questions.text: Which of the following are Directive Principles of State Policy? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Agricultural promotion, environment, and child labor are Directive Principles. Rights are Fundamental Rights, not Directives.

questions.text: Assertion: The Constitution establishes India as a welfare state. Reason: It includes Directive Principles that guide the state toward social and economic justice.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Directive Principles outline the welfare objectives of the state.

questions.text: How many times has the Indian Constitution been amended?
questions.explanation: As of recent times, the Constitution has been amended over 100 times to address changing needs.

questions.text: Match constitutional amendments with their significance:
questions.explanation: These amendments brought significant changes to India's constitutional framework.

questions.text: The Constituent Assembly adopted the Constitution on November 26, 1949.
questions.explanation: The Constituent Assembly adopted the Constitution on November 26, 1949, known as Constitution Day.

questions.text: The ______ is the ceremonial head of state in India while the Prime Minister is the executive head.
questions.explanation: The President of India holds the ceremonial position; the PM exercises executive power.
